does: This paper systematically evaluates how well reward models can capture human preferences in story generation and introduces two key contributions: StoryRMB, a benchmark dataset of 1,133 human-verified story preference instances with prompts, chosen stories, and rejected alternatives, and StoryReward, a specialized reward model trained on approximately 100,000 story preference pairs. The authors find that existing reward models perform poorly on story preference prediction, with the best achieving only 66.3% accuracy on their benchmark. StoryReward is developed specifically to address this gap by learning from diverse story preference data across multiple domains, aiming to better capture the subjective, nuanced aspects of human narrative preferences that general-purpose reward models miss.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of reward modeling and language generation, extending beyond simple text quality assessment to the specialized domain of narrative structure and coherence. It responds to recent advances in using reward models to align LLMs with human preferences, particularly in the context of RLHF and preference learning frameworks. The paper connects to prior work on story generation evaluation and human preference annotation, while acknowledging limitations in existing general-purpose reward models when applied to the complex, subjective nature of storytelling. It extends the paradigm of domain-specific reward models by focusing specifically on narrative preferences, combining insights from preference learning literature with the unique challenges of evaluating coherent, structured narratives.
enables: This work opens pathways for improved alignment of story-generating systems with human preferences through better preference modeling. StoryReward and StoryRMB enable future research on using specialized reward models for RLHF training in narrative generation, potentially leading to LLMs that produce more human-aligned, structurally coherent stories. The benchmark facilitates evaluation of future story generation models and preference modeling approaches. More broadly, this work points toward domain-specific reward models as a general strategy for capturing task-specific human preferences, suggesting similar specialized approaches could benefit other creative writing tasks. The constructed preference dataset and methodology could support downstream applications in automated storytelling systems, interactive narrative generation, and personalized story recommendation systems.
